survey_result:
Self‑Supervised Learning: A Comprehensive Survey of Contrastive Paradigms, Computer Vision Applications, and Video Representation Methods
The rapid emergence of self‑supervised learning (SSL) over the past decade has reshaped representation learning by enabling models to extract semantically meaningful features from unlabeled data. At its core, SSL leverages intrinsic structures or surrogate tasks to supervise representation learning, thereby reducing or eliminating the dependence on manual annotation. This survey examines key threads in self‑supervised learning before 2023, focusing first on the evolution of contrastive learning and its progression from negative‑sample‑based objectives to techniques that forgo negatives; next on the breadth of computer vision applications that have adopted SSL formulations; and finally on the distinct approaches to self‑supervised video representation learning that exploit temporal, motion, multi‑modal, and spatial–temporal coherence cues.
Contrastive Learning: From Negative Samples to Negative‑Free Objectives
Early contrastive self‑supervised learning built on the fundamental idea of pulling together positive pairs—typically different views or augmentations of the same sample—while repelling negative pairs drawn from other instances in the dataset. One of the pioneering frameworks in this regime was
SimCLR
(“A simple framework for contrastive learning of visual representations”), which formalized the combination of strong data augmentation, a projection head, and an InfoNCE loss to maximize agreement between positive views while contrasting against a large set of negatives. Together with
MoCo
(“Momentum contrast for unsupervised visual representation learning”), which introduced a dynamic memory bank and momentum encoder to maintain a consistent set of negative examples across training iterations, these methods anchored the field in the necessity of negative instances for meaningful representation separation. They demonstrated that with careful design of augmentations and large negative sets, the resulting representations not only clustered semantically related inputs but also transferred effectively to downstream tasks.
Despite their efficacy, negative sample‑based contrastive methods brought practical challenges. The reliance on large numbers of negatives imposed heavy memory and computational demands, and the sampling of “hard” yet representative negatives remained a delicate balance. These considerations motivated research into
negative‑free paradigms
, which aim to maintain positive consistency without explicit repulsion against sampled negatives. Techniques such as
BYOL (Bootstrap Your Own Latent)
and
SimSiam
showed that a carefully engineered interplay between an online and a target network, along with mechanisms like stop‑gradient, could avoid collapse even without negatives by implicitly structuring the representation space to preserve variance and invariance to augmentations. Complementary to these siamese architectures, methods like
Barlow Twins
and
VICReg (Variance‑Invariance‑Covariance Regularization)
reframed the problem from a pairwise contrastive objective to one of redundancy reduction and feature decorrelation. Barlow Twins explicitly penalized cross‑correlations between components of the two views, encouraging each dimension to capture distinct information, while VICReg combined invariance with variance and covariance regularization to maintain feature diversity without negatives. This negative‑free shift represented a significant conceptual evolution: rather than relying on negative examples to enforce separation, these methods sculpted feature statistics to achieve rich, discriminative embeddings that remain aligned across views.
Across this spectrum—from early SimCLR and MoCo variants rooted in Noise‑Contrastive Estimation and memory bank dynamics, through predictive siamese models like BYOL and SimSiam, to redundancy reduction frameworks—one observes an arc of innovation driven by the twin goals of computational efficiency and robust, transferable representations. These developments illustrated that the core requirement of negative examples could be relaxed under appropriate architectural and loss function designs, thereby broadening the applicability and scalability of self‑supervised contrastive learning.
Applications of Self‑Supervised Learning in Computer Vision
Self‑supervised paradigms have been applied widely across computer vision tasks, frequently by defining carefully crafted surrogate objectives that exploit domain‑specific invariances or structures. In
image processing and feature learning
, early works such as “Unsupervised learning of visual representations by solving jigsaw puzzles” and “Unsupervised representation learning by predicting image rotations” established the principle that pretext tasks based on geometric transformations could drive the network to learn semantic features. Jigsaw puzzles required the model to predict the correct arrangement of shuffled patches, implicitly capturing object parts and global context, while rotation prediction instilled an understanding of orientation and structural regularities. Related pixel‑level tasks like edge detection (“Unsupervised learning of edges”) or colorization (“Colorful image colorization”, “Learning representations for automatic colorization”) leveraged innate image properties—such as edge continuity or color statistics—to induce representations beneficial for downstream recognition tasks. These pretext tasks connected low‑level vision structure with high‑level semantic comprehension.
Other efforts targeted
object‑level analysis
by exploiting object motion and statistics within visual streams. For instance, embedding basic motion cues into representation learning encouraged models to disentangle object shape from appearance changes. In reinforcement learning and robotic perception domains, self‑supervised “Supersizing self‑supervision: Learning to grasp from 50k tries and 700 robot hours” demonstrated that proxy tasks derived from robotic interaction outcomes could effectively guide feature learning in the absence of explicit labels, underpinning control and manipulation efficacy.
Beyond static images, self‑supervised techniques have been adapted to handle
multi‑modal data interactions
. Methods like matching co‑located aerial imagery with audio recordings illustrated how embedding images and audio into a joint space could capture scene attributes that correlate across modalities, a critical capability in remote sensing where annotated data is sparse. Likewise, approaches that align visual features with textual or semantic representations highlight the extensibility of self‑supervision to cross‑modal correspondence.
In ensemble, these applications underscore the versatility of self‑supervised learning: by aligning intrinsic structures—whether geometric transformations, motion statistics, cross‑channel predictions, or multi‑modal correspondences—with representational objectives, SSL methods consistently deliver features that generalize across downstream tasks ranging from object recognition to scene understanding and robotic control.
Video Self‑Supervised Learning: Temporal, Motion, Multi‑Modal, and Spatial–Temporal Coherence
The temporal richness of video data presents unique opportunities and challenges for SSL, prompting methodologies that exploit time as an intrinsic supervisory signal. A significant class of early video SSL methods hinged on
temporal ordering
tasks: by presenting frames or clips in shuffled sequences, models learned to infer natural temporal progression, as exemplified by “Shuffle and learn: unsupervised learning using temporal order verification” and “Self‑supervised spatiotemporal learning via video clip order prediction”. These tasks encouraged representations sensitive to temporal context, implicitly encoding dynamic scene evolution.
Complementing sequence order, playback attributes such as
playback speed and direction
formed another axis of supervision. For instance, the “Learning and using the arrow of time” paradigm challenged models to distinguish forward from reversed sequences, thereby anchoring temporal directionality within the learned features. Such tasks pushed models to discern subtle motion cues that purely spatial objectives might overlook.
Other methods instead concentrated on
motion information
directly by embedding motion statistics or predictive frameworks into visual representations. “Self‑supervised spatio‑temporal representation learning for videos by predicting motion and appearance statistics” targeted both spatial and temporal modalities by regressing frame appearance alongside motion descriptors, thereby binding motion dynamics to representational learning. Contrastive approaches specialized for video, such as spatiotemporal contrastive learning, extended image contrastive objectives across time by contrasting augmented clips from the same video against others while maintaining temporal consistency. Graph‑based schemes like Temporal Contrastive Graph Learning structured temporal dependencies explicitly across different time scales to capture nuanced motion relationships between segments.
Beyond unimodal cues,
multi‑modal video SSL
exploited audio–visual synchronization to enrich representations. Audio‑visual contrastive models learned to associate temporally aligned frames and sounds, using video–audio pairs as supervisory signals that anchored visual representations to corresponding acoustic context, a strategy which simultaneously enhances robustness in both modalities. Such approaches illustrate how cross‑modal temporal consistency can act as a powerful self‑supervisory signal, uniting heterogeneous data streams into cohesive embeddings.
Finally, methods that leverage
spatial–temporal coherence
seek to understand persistent object identities and correspondences across frames. By enforcing consistency of object representations over temporal transformations, these techniques enable tracking and correspondence learning without labels, thus supporting downstream tasks like segmentation and motion capture. Through learning to associate spatial features across time, models glean information about object permanence and scene structure that transcends individual frames.
Collectively, these strands of video self‑supervised learning highlight the multifaceted nature of temporal information. Whether through sequence ordering, motion regression, multi‑modal synchronization, or long‑range spatial–temporal coherence, SSL approaches have harnessed the intrinsic structure of video data to derive rich, generalizable representations. These representations not only excel in traditional tasks like action recognition and retrieval but also equip models for fine‑grained temporal understanding such as object tracking and temporal correspondence.
In synthesizing these developments, a unifying theme emerges: self‑supervised learning thrives when intrinsic data structure can stand in for manual supervision. From negative sample contrastive paradigms to negative‑free objectives, from geometric image pretexts to motion and temporal cues in video, SSL has matured into a cornerstone methodology for representation learning across modalities and domains. The interplay between surrogate tasks and architectural innovation continues to propel the field, offering a pathway toward more autonomous and scalable learning systems.
general_prompt: Please write a comprehensive survey paper on self-supervised learning based on the provided papers.

checklist.group_name: Introduction and motivation of self-supervised learning

requirements.content: The article should explain that deep supervised learning algorithms typically require large volumes of labeled data to achieve satisfactory performance, which creates a fundamental limitation.
The article should identify that the process of collecting and labeling data is expensive and time-consuming, requiring skilled human annotators with domain expertise.
The article should provide concrete examples of domains where labeling is costly, such as medical diagnosis requiring expert radiologists or web user profiling requiring experienced assessors.
The article should mention that supervised learning methods are vulnerable to spurious correlations, generalization errors, and adversarial attacks.
The article should define self-supervised learning as a method that aims to learn discriminative features from unlabeled data without relying on human-annotated labels.
The article should explain that SSL is a subset of unsupervised learning that generates output labels intrinsically from input data examples.
The article should define pretext tasks as surrogate or proxy tasks that are not the primary objective but serve as means to generate robust pre-trained models.
The article should explain that pseudo-labels in SSL are automatically generated based on specific attributes of the input data without human annotations.
The article should describe the general SSL pipeline consisting of a self-supervised pre-training phase followed by transfer to downstream tasks.
The article should explain that in the pre-training phase, a pre-defined pretext task is formulated for deep learning algorithms to solve using pseudo-labels.
The article should clarify that after pre-training, the acquired model can be transferred to downstream tasks through fine-tuning or direct evaluation.
The article should highlight that SSL algorithms can leverage extensive unlabeled data since pseudo-label generation does not require human annotations.
The article should mention that SSL has demonstrated promising outcomes with reduced performance disparity compared to supervised algorithms in downstream tasks.
The article should position SSL as one of several machine learning paradigms developed to address supervised learning limitations, alongside active learning and semi-supervised learning.
The article should acknowledge that SSL has garnered significant attention in the research community and is recognized as a crucial element for achieving human-level intelligence.
The article should demonstrate the rapid growth of SSL research by providing evidence of substantial publication volumes or research activity in recent years.

checklist.group_name: Context-based pretext tasks and methods

requirements.content: The article should define context-based methods as relying on inherent contextual relationships among provided examples, encompassing aspects such as spatial structures and preservation of both local and global consistency.
The article should explain that context-based pretext tasks are designed for networks to solve where supervised signals are derived from the data itself through self-supervision.

The article should describe rotation-based methods that train deep neural networks by recognizing random geometric transformations, specifically using rotations of 0°, 90°, 180°, and 270° to generate additional images from each original image.

The article should explain that rotation methods employ rotation angles as self-supervised labels using a set of K=4 geometric transformations and train a CNN to perform rotation prediction through a four-class categorization task.

The article should present the mathematical formulation where the loss function for rotation prediction is defined as the negative log-likelihood of predicting the correct rotation angle across all possible rotations.

The article should describe colorization methods that utilize the lightness channel of color images as input and the corresponding ab color channels in the CIE Lab color space as self-supervised signals.

The article should explain that colorization tasks predict the ab color channels given an input lightness channel, with the objective commonly formulated as minimizing the Frobenius norm between predicted and ground truth color channels.
The article should mention that colorization offers the advantage of requiring freely available training data since any color image can be converted to grayscale and used for training.
The article should describe jigsaw methods that fragment images into discrete patches, randomly rearrange their positions, and train models to reconstruct the original spatial order of the patches.
The article should explain that jigsaw approaches operate under the assumption that models accomplish these tasks by comprehending the contextual information embedded within the image patches.
The article should mention that jigsaw puzzle performance exhibits log-linear growth in relation to data size and improves with higher-capacity models and increased problem complexity.
The article should mention relative patch location prediction as another context-based pretext task that learns spatial relationships by predicting the relative positions of image patches.

The article should discuss pretext-invariant representation learning approaches that enable learning of invariant representations through pretext tasks, addressing the issue that many pretext tasks yield representations covariant with image transformations.

The article should acknowledge that context-based approaches exhibit limited applicability due to their inferior performance compared to more recent contrastive learning and masked image modeling methods.

The article should explain that context-based methods served as important early developments in self-supervised learning but have been largely superseded by contrastive learning and generative approaches in terms of downstream task performance.

checklist.group_name: Contrastive learning algorithms and variants

sub_groups.sub_group_name: Core Contrastive Learning Principles and Loss Functions

requirements.content: The article should explain that contrastive learning follows the principle of promoting proximity between positive examples while maximizing separation between negative examples in the latent space.
The article should present the InfoNCE loss function formulation with the specific mathematical expression involving temperature parameter τ and the exponential terms for positive and negative pairs.
The article should explain that contrastive learning is typically framed as an instance discrimination task where different augmented views of the same instance are treated as positive pairs.
The article should describe how data augmentation techniques such as cropping, resizing, and color distortion are crucial for generating different views in contrastive learning.
The article should describe MoCo's approach of framing contrastive learning as a dictionary look-up task where a query matches with one positive key and is contrasted against negative keys.
The article should explain that MoCo uses a momentum encoder to generate features stored in a feature queue as negative examples that do not undergo gradient updates during backpropagation.

The article should describe SimCLR's mini-batch sampling strategy where for a batch of N instances generating 2N augmented instances, the remaining 2(N-1) augmented instances serve as negatives for any given positive pair.

The article should highlight the key difference between MoCo and SimCLR: MoCo uses a feature queue with momentum updates while SimCLR uses current mini-batch negatives that all undergo gradient updates.

The article should explain that BYOL eliminates the need for negative pairs by using two identical networks called Siamese networks with different weights: an online network and a target network updated through gradual averaging.

The article should describe SimSiam's use of a stop-gradient operation that treats one branch's output as a constant during loss computation to prevent collapse without requiring negative examples or momentum encoders.
The article should explain that both BYOL and SimSiam employ asymmetric architectures where only one branch has a prediction head while the other serves as the target.

The article should describe Barlow Twins' approach of computing a cross-correlation matrix between two batches of embeddings and minimizing off-diagonal elements while maximizing diagonal elements to achieve decorrelation.

The article should explain VICReg's three-component approach combining variance regularization using hinge loss on standard deviation, invariance through mean-squared Euclidean distance, and covariance regularization to prevent dimensional collapse.

The article should clarify that VICReg applies variance and covariance regularization independently to each branch of the Siamese architecture, differing from Barlow Twins which uses cross-correlation between branches.
The article should mention that minimizing contrastive loss effectively maximizes a lower bound of the mutual information between different views of the same instance.
The article should explain the connection between contrastive learning and spectral clustering, where learned representations correspond to embeddings of a positive pair graph in spectral clustering.
The article should describe how contrastive learning relates to Principal Component Analysis in the context of deep linear networks, where the maximization objective recovers optimal PCA solutions.

checklist.group_name: Masked image modeling and generative approaches

requirements.content: The article should explain that MIM is a variant of the denoising autoencoder (DAE), establishing the foundational connection between these approaches.
The article should identify BERT and GPT as renowned variants of the DAE that achieved remarkable success in NLP, motivating the transfer to computer vision.
The article should explain that MIM leverages co-occurrence relationships among image patches as supervision signals, distinguishing it from other self-supervised approaches.
The article should mention that ViT marked a pivotal moment for applying MIM to computer vision, enabling the transformer architecture to be effectively used for visual tasks.

The article should describe BEiT as employing a tokenization procedure that breaks down input images into visual tokens using a discrete variational autoencoder (dVAE) to create a predefined visual vocabulary.
The article should explain that MAE approaches the problem from the perspective of image signal sparsity, necessitating higher masking rates such as 75%, without utilizing image tokens.

The article should present the formal definition of MIM as MIM := L(D(E(T₁(I))), T₂(I)), where E denotes the encoder, D denotes the decoder, T₁ represents input transformation, and T₂ represents target label transformation.
The article should distinguish between BEiT's two-stage approach (first training a tokenizer, then BERT-style training) and MAE's one-stage end-to-end approach with a decoder.

The article should categorize MIM methods based on reconstruction targets into low-level targets (raw pixels, HOG features), high-level targets (VQ-VAE, VQ-GAN), self-distillation, and contrastive/multi-modal teacher approaches.
The article should identify that the central challenge in MIM lies in the selection of the target representation T₂, which leads to different categorizations of MIM methods.
The article should mention iBOT as an online tokenizer adaptation of BEiT that addresses the limitation of dVAE in capturing only low-level semantics within local details.

The article should describe Context Autoencoder (CAE) as introducing an alignment constraint to encourage masked patch representations to lie in the encoded representation space, decoupling representation learning from the pretext task.
The article should mention VideoMAE as extending MAE to videos and discovering that extremely high masking ratios (90% to 95%) are permissible in video mask modeling.
The article should note that MIM-based pre-trained models can be fine-tuned to achieve superior performance in most downstream tasks compared to contrastive learning approaches.

The article should explain that MIM-based methods have more efficient resource utilization compared to contrastive learning, possibly due to the absence of example interactions, allowing them to scale up models and data more effectively.

checklist.group_name: Contrastive generative hybrid methods

sub_groups.sub_group_name: Motivation and Rationale for Hybrid Approaches

requirements.content: The article should explain that contrastive models are data-hungry and vulnerable to overfitting issues, while generative models encounter data-filling challenges and exhibit inferior data scaling capabilities when compared to contrastive models.

The article should describe that contrastive models often focus on global views while overlooking internal structures within images, whereas masked image modeling primarily models local relationships.

The article should mention that contrastive methods show substantial performance improvements as data increases and demonstrate remarkable generalization without additional fine-tuning, while generative methods are more adept at handling low-data scenarios.

The article should describe joint training approaches that simultaneously perform contrastive learning and masked image modeling tasks, such as CMAE which introduces pixel shifting for generating plausible positive views and a feature decoder for enhancing contrastive pair features.

The article should explain approaches that use contrastive learning views as reconstruction targets for generative methods, exemplified by SiameseIM which utilizes views generated by contrastive learning as the target for MIM reconstruction in latent space.

The article should describe generative-guided contrastive learning approaches, such as RECON, which trains generative modeling to guide contrastive learning thereby leveraging benefits of both paradigms.

The article should mention iBOT as a method that draws inspiration from both BEiT and DINO, modifying the tokenizer of BEiT to an online distilled teacher while integrating cross-view distillation from the DINO framework.

The article should describe RePre as a method that integrates local feature learning into self-supervised vision transformers through reconstructive pre-training by incorporating an additional branch dedicated to reconstructing raw image pixels alongside the contrastive objective.

The article should mention approaches that employ GANs for online data augmentation in contrastive learning, such as methods that devise contrastive modules learning view-invariant features for generation with view-invariant loss functions.

The article should acknowledge that naive combinations of contrastive and generative approaches may not always yield performance gains and can even perform worse than generative model baselines, potentially exacerbating representation over-fitting issues.

The article should explain that performance degradation in hybrid methods can be attributed to disparate properties of contrastive learning and generative methods, such as contrastive learning methods typically exhibiting longer attention distances while generative methods tend to favor local attention.

The article should explain that hybrid methods aim to leverage the data scaling advantages of contrastive learning while maintaining the local modeling capabilities and low-data performance of generative approaches.

The article should describe how contrastive generative methods address the limitations of individual approaches, such as contrastive methods' tendency to overfit with limited data and generative methods' challenges with data scaling beyond certain magnitudes.

checklist.group_name: Integration with other learning paradigms

requirements.content: The article should explain that Self-supervised GANs (SS-GAN) combine the standard GAN objective function with self-supervised rotation prediction tasks, where the discriminator learns both to distinguish real from fake data and to predict rotation angles of input images.

The article should describe that SS-GAN incorporates rotation invariance into the generation process by adding a rotation prediction loss term with rotation angles as self-supervised labels to both generator and discriminator objective functions.

The article should explain that Self-supervised semi-supervised learning (S4L) combines labeled and unlabeled data by optimizing a joint objective function that includes both classification loss on labeled data and self-supervised loss on unlabeled data.

The article should describe how self-supervised learning serves as an auxiliary task in semi-supervised settings, where SSL pretext tasks provide additional supervision signals when labeled data is limited.

The article should explain that multiview contrastive learning treats different views of the same sample as positive examples, with methods like CLIP using contrastive learning to predict correspondences between images and text captions.

The article should describe how self-supervised learning leverages cross-modal correspondences, such as using co-occurrence relationships between visual and audio signals in videos, or between images and their textual descriptions.

The article should mention that self-supervised learning has been extended to 3D data like point clouds, where cross-view and cross-modality correspondences between 2D images and 3D point clouds are used for joint representation learning.

The article should explain that Multi-instance learning NCE (MIL-NCE) extends the InfoNCE loss for multi-instance scenarios, where positive pairs come from the same bag and negative pairs come from different bags.

The article should describe how MIL-NCE operates on sets of positive and negative video-narration pairs, maximizing similarity within positive sets while minimizing similarity between positive and negative sets.

The article should explain that Test Time Training (TTT) with self-supervision converts individual unlabeled test examples into self-supervised learning problems, updating model parameters before making predictions to handle distribution shifts.

The article should describe that TTT with MAE treats test-time adaptation as a one-sample learning problem, where the model is optimized on masked reconstruction of each test input to achieve better bias-variance tradeoff under distribution shifts.

The article should mention that self-supervised learning has been integrated with diverse machine learning paradigms including clustering, transfer learning, few-shot learning, neural architecture search, and reinforcement learning.

The article should explain that adversarial training can be enhanced by combining supervised cross-entropy loss with self-supervised loss on adversarially perturbed examples, improving model robustness against adversarial attacks.

checklist.group_name: Applications in computer vision and image processing

requirements.content: The article should explain how self-supervised learning is applied to image inpainting tasks, where models learn to predict missing or masked regions of images using context from surrounding pixels.
The article should describe the application of SSL to image denoising, where models learn to remove noise from corrupted images without requiring pairs of clean and noisy training images.
The article should discuss how SSL enables super-resolution tasks by learning to enhance image resolution through self-supervised objectives rather than requiring high-resolution ground truth images.
The article should explain how colorization tasks use SSL by predicting color channels from grayscale inputs, typically converting from lightness channel to ab color channels in CIE Lab color space.

The article should describe how SSL is applied to object detection tasks, where pre-trained self-supervised features are transferred to improve detection performance without requiring additional labeled bounding box data during pre-training.
The article should explain how semantic image segmentation benefits from SSL pre-training, where models learn pixel-level representations that can be fine-tuned for dense prediction tasks.

The article should discuss the application of SSL to person re-identification tasks, where models learn discriminative features to match person identities across different camera views without requiring identity labels during pre-training.
The article should describe how SSL is used for optical flow prediction, where models learn to estimate pixel-level motion between consecutive frames using temporal consistency as supervision.

The article should explain how SSL leverages temporal information in videos, specifically describing how frame order prediction tasks train models to determine the correct temporal sequence of shuffled video frames.

The article should describe how video playback direction serves as a self-supervised signal, where models learn to distinguish between forward and backward video sequences to understand temporal causality.

The article should discuss how multi-modal data in videos enables SSL through audio-visual correspondence, where models learn joint representations by exploiting the natural synchronization between visual and auditory streams.

The article should explain how spatial-temporal coherence in videos provides supervision through cycle consistency, where models learn visual correspondence by tracking objects across time using temporal consistency constraints.

The article should describe Contrastive Predictive Coding (CPC) as a universal framework that extends autoregressive prediction from sequential data to images by learning representations through latent space predictions.

The article should explain how Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture enables SSL by applying transformer models to sequences of image patches, demonstrating outstanding performance in image recognition tasks.

The article should discuss how iGPT investigates autoregressive and denoising objectives for images, exploring whether GPT-like models can effectively learn visual representations similar to their success in natural language processing.

checklist.group_name: Applications in video understanding and analysis

requirements.content: The article should explain that frame order can be used as a self-supervised signal, where models learn to predict the correct temporal sequence of shuffled video frames or clips.
The article should mention that video playback direction serves as supervision, where models learn to distinguish between forward and backward video playback to understand the arrow of time.
The article should describe that video playback speed can be utilized as a pretext task, where models predict whether objects move faster or slower than normal speed.

The article should mention odd-one-out learning as a video SSL method, where the task is to identify the video subsequence with incorrect temporal frame order among related elements with correct temporal order.
The article should explain that cycle consistency in time can serve as a self-supervised signal for learning visual correspondence in unlabeled videos.
The article should mention that spatial-temporal consistency of depth maps can be utilized to mitigate forgetting during video representation learning.
The article should describe that dynamic motion filters can be employed to enhance motion representations for improving human action recognition in videos.
The article should mention video cloze procedure (VCP) as a self-supervised method that learns rich spatial-temporal representations by masking and predicting video content.
The article should explain that audio and visual components in videos are intrinsically interconnected and can be leveraged through temporal synchronization for self-supervised learning.
The article should mention that tri-modal approaches combining vision, audio, and language in videos have been explored for self-supervised representation learning.
The article should describe that multi-viewpoint videos can be used for learning robotic behaviors and representations through self-supervised techniques.

The article should mention CoCLR (Cooperative Learning for videos with Contrastive Learning and Representation learning) as a video SSL method that bears similarities to SimCLR but is adapted for video data.

The article should explain that masked autoencoder (MAE) has been extended to video representation learning, demonstrating that spatiotemporal co-occurrence of videos can outperform supervised training.
The article should mention that asymmetrically modeling spatiotemporal information is important for video SSL, as temporal dimension is unique compared to spatial dimensions.
The article should describe memory-augmented self-supervised methods for video tracking that enable generalizable and accurate pixel-level tracking across video sequences.

checklist.group_name: Applications in natural language processing

requirements.content: The article should discuss the continuous bag-of-words model and continuous skip-gram model as pioneering works for performing SSL on word embeddings in NLP
The article should explain that generative self-supervised learning algorithms have long dominated the field of NLP, fundamentally based on language models employing maximum likelihood estimation

The article should mention ELECTRA as a pioneer discriminative algorithm that employs the Replaced Token Detection (RTD) task and draws upon the structure and ideas of GANs without adopting GAN's training paradigm
The article should describe that supervised contrastive pretraining enables zero-shot prediction of unseen text classes and enhances few-shot performance
The article should explain that task-agnostic self-supervised contrastive pre-training has been shown to improve language modeling performance
The article should identify that the automatic creation of textual input augmentation remains a significant challenge because a single token can reverse the meaning of a sentence
The article should explain why discriminative algorithms were initially deemed ineffective in NLP due to the distinct semantics inherent in language
The article should mention BERT and GPT as early foundational works in self-supervised learning for NLP
The article should reference the evolution from early works like BERT and GPT to recent trillion-scale large language models
The article should state that generative SSL algorithms continue to dominate NLP despite attempts by discriminative approaches to challenge conventions

checklist.group_name: Applications in other domains (medical, remote sensing)

requirements.content: The article should explain that the medical field typically has limited availability of labeled data while possessing vast amounts of unlabeled data, making SSL a compelling approach for medical applications.
The article should identify medical image segmentation as a specific task where SSL has been effectively employed in the medical domain.
The article should mention 3D medical image analysis as an application area where SSL methods have been utilized.

checklist.group_name: Performance comparison and evaluation metrics

requirements.content: The survey should specify that models are typically pre-trained on ImageNet-1k and evaluated on downstream tasks including semantic segmentation on PASCAL VOC, COCO, and ADE20k datasets, as well as object detection tasks on VOC and COCO datasets.

The survey should distinguish between two main evaluation protocols: linear probe evaluation where only a linear classifier is trained on frozen features, and fine-tuning evaluation where the entire pre-trained model is fine-tuned on downstream tasks.

The survey should mention that different backbone architectures are used in comparisons, specifically noting ResNet-50 and ViT-B/16 as common choices, with results sometimes reported for AlexNet when other architectures are unavailable.
The survey should specify standard evaluation metrics including mean average precision (mAP) with AP50 for PASCAL VOC detection, bounding-box AP and mask AP for COCO detection and segmentation tasks.

The survey should include comprehensive comparison tables showing numerical performance results across multiple SSL methods on both linear probe and fine-tuning tasks, with results for random initialization and supervised pre-training as baselines.

The survey should present performance results for video understanding tasks, including evaluation on datasets like Kinetics, Something Something-v2, and AVA, with methods pre-trained on video datasets.

The survey should include performance comparisons across different categories of SSL methods including context-based methods, contrastive learning variants, masked image modeling approaches, and contrastive generative methods.
The survey should report specific numerical results showing that supervised ResNet-50 achieves 76.5% on ImageNet linear probe and that ViT-B supervised baseline achieves 82.3% accuracy.

The survey should identify that contrastive learning methods typically achieve superior linear probe performance compared to other SSL approaches, attributed to generating well-structured latent spaces with effective category separation.

The survey should note that masked image modeling methods generally achieve better fine-tuning performance compared to contrastive learning methods, while contrastive learning methods are more susceptible to overfitting during fine-tuning.

The survey should explain that MIM-based approaches consistently show substantial performance improvements in downstream tasks, while contrastive learning methods offer comparatively limited assistance for transfer learning.

The survey should discuss that contrastive learning methods tend to employ resource-intensive techniques like momentum encoders, memory queues, and multi-crop, significantly increasing computing and storage demands compared to MIM methods.

The survey should categorize SSL methods by computational complexity, distinguishing between encoder-only models, encoder-decoder models, momentum encoder approaches, and siamese models in terms of computational load hierarchy.

The survey should identify that MIM-based methods have more efficient resource utilization compared to contrastive learning methods, possibly due to the absence of example interactions, allowing easier scaling of models and data.

The survey should note that methods requiring large batch training or employing multi-crop techniques demand substantial memory resources, with specific mention of batch size requirements varying significantly across different SSL approaches.

The survey should explain that the primary sources of time complexity and memory consumption are the neural network computations rather than SSL-specific components, such as cross-correlation matrix calculations in methods like Barlow Twins.

checklist.group_name: Theoretical analysis of self-supervised learning

requirements.content: The article should establish the connection between contrastive learning and principal component analysis, explaining how contrastive learning with InfoNCE loss can be formulated as a max-min problem where the max function corresponds to PCA in deep linear networks.

The article should explain the relationship between contrastive learning and spectral clustering, demonstrating how representations obtained from contrastive learning correspond to embeddings of a positive pair graph in spectral clustering.

The article should discuss the population augmentation graph concept where nodes represent augmented data and edges exist between nodes from the same original example, with the assumption that different classes have limited connections.

The article should explain that minimizing contrastive loss effectively maximizes a lower bound of the mutual information between two views, establishing the information theoretic foundation of contrastive methods.

The article should present the principle for designing data augmentation based on information theory, which involves maximizing mutual information between views and task labels while minimizing shared information between input views.

The article should explain the InfoNCE loss function derivation from noise contrastive estimation and its role as a form of contrastive loss that promotes similarity between positive pairs while separating negative pairs.

The article should discuss the theoretical foundation behind feature decorrelation methods like Barlow Twins, explaining how cross-correlation matrix optimization encourages diagonal elements to be close to 1 while off-diagonal elements approach 0.

The article should analyze why self-distillation methods like BYOL and SimSiam avoid collapse without negative examples, explaining the role of stop-gradient operations and momentum updates in preventing trivial solutions.

The article should discuss theoretical investigations showing that when representation dimensionality exceeds the maximum count of disjoint subgraphs in the augmentation graph, linear classification using learned representations guarantees minimal error.

The article should explain the theoretical understanding of why self-supervised pre-training performs effectively with limited labeled data but may not surpass supervised learning when sufficient labeled data is available.

The article should address the theoretical challenge of preventing representation collapse in self-supervised learning, explaining mechanisms like asymmetric networks, momentum encoders, and stop-gradient operations that maintain meaningful representations.

checklist.group_name: Future trends and research directions

requirements.content: The article should identify the need for theoretical unification of different SSL paradigms, specifically mentioning that understanding how to unify contrastive learning methods with masked image modeling approaches remains an open theoretical challenge.

The article should discuss the connection between contrastive learning and spectral clustering, where representations obtained from contrastive learning correspond to embeddings of a positive pair graph in spectral clustering.

The article should mention that contrastive learning can be connected to principal component analysis in the context of deep linear networks, where the max function in representation learning is equivalent to PCA.

The article should identify the challenge of automatic design of optimal pretext tasks for enhancing performance on specific downstream tasks, noting that this problem remains insufficiently resolved despite methods like pixel-to-propagation consistency.

The article should discuss the need for developing methods to determine the most suitable SSL algorithm for particular circumstances, as there is no universally applicable solution and the optimal selection should align with specific problem structures.

The article should identify the trend toward unified SSL paradigms across multiple modalities, noting that masked image modeling has shown progress similar to masked language models in NLP, suggesting possibilities for cross-modal unification.

The article should mention that vision transformers bridge the gap between visual and verbal modalities, enabling construction of unified transformer models for both computer vision and natural language processing tasks.

The article should discuss the connection between SSL and multi-modality learning in the context of infant cognitive processes, where both methodologies share resemblances with how infants learn through observational and comparative processes.

The article should question whether SSL can effectively leverage vast amounts of unlabeled data and identify the need to determine theoretical inflection points for when additional unlabeled data ceases to provide benefits.

The article should challenge the assumption that unlabeled data invariably leads to improved outcomes, noting that performance degradation can arise when model assumptions fail to align with underlying problem structure, referencing the no free lunch theorem.

The article should identify that contrastive learning methods tend to employ resource-intensive techniques like momentum encoders and memory queues, while masked image modeling approaches have more efficient resource utilization due to absence of example interactions.

The article should note the trend that masked image modeling approaches consistently exhibit substantial performance enhancements in downstream tasks through fine-tuning, while contrastive learning methods offer comparatively limited assistance in fine-tuning scenarios.

checklist.group_name: Open problems and challenges

requirements.content: The article should identify the lack of theoretical understanding as a major challenge in self-supervised learning, specifically mentioning the need to understand how different SSL algorithms work and how to unify them conceptually.

The article should discuss the challenge of establishing theoretical connections between contrastive learning methods and masked image modeling methods, acknowledging these as two distinct paradigms that need unification.

The article should identify the automatic design of optimal pretext tasks for specific downstream tasks as an open research problem, noting that current solutions like pixel-to-propagation consistency methods remain insufficient.
The article should acknowledge that the challenge of automatically selecting or designing pretext tasks requires further theoretical investigation and remains incompletely resolved.

The article should identify the need for a unified self-supervised learning paradigm that encompasses multiple modalities as a key research direction, noting the potential for bridging computer vision and natural language processing.

The article should discuss how self-supervised learning and multi-modality learning can be synergistically integrated, drawing parallels to infant cognitive processes and identifying this as an open research question.

The article should raise the question of whether self-supervised learning can effectively leverage vast amounts of unlabeled data and how to determine theoretical inflection points for data scaling benefits.

The article should challenge the assumption that unlabeled data invariably leads to improved outcomes, referencing the no free lunch theorem and discussing scenarios where performance degradation can occur when model assumptions fail to align with problem structure.

The article should identify the challenge of determining the most optimal self-supervised learning algorithm for specific scenarios, noting that there is no universally applicable solution and that algorithm selection should align with specific problem structures.

The article should propose the development of practical guidelines or checklists to help users identify the most suitable self-supervised learning method under particular circumstances as a promising avenue for future research.

checklist.group_name: Video Self-Supervised Learning Methods by Information Type

requirements.content: Video self-supervised learning methods should explain that temporal information in videos encompasses frame order, video playback direction, video playback speed, and future prediction information.

Video self-supervised learning methods should describe Misra et al.'s approach for learning visual representations from raw spatiotemporal signals and determining the correct temporal sequence of frames extracted from videos.

Video self-supervised learning methods should explain Fernando et al.'s odd-one-out learning approach where the objective is to identify the unrelated or odd element within a set of related elements, with the odd element corresponding to a video subsequence with an incorrect temporal frame order.

Video self-supervised learning methods should describe Lee et al.'s method of using temporally shuffled frames presented in non-chronological order as inputs to train a CNN for predicting the correct order of shuffled sequences.

Video self-supervised learning methods should explain Xu et al.'s approach of utilizing temporally shuffled clips as inputs instead of individual frames to train 3D CNNs for sorting these shuffled clips.

Video self-supervised learning methods should describe Wei et al.'s temporal direction analysis involving discerning the arrow of time to determine if a video sequence progresses in forward or backward direction.

Video self-supervised learning methods should explain Benaim et al.'s SpeedNet approach focused on predicting the speeds of moving objects in videos to determine whether they moved faster or slower than normal speed.

Video self-supervised learning methods should describe Yao et al.'s method of leveraging playback rates and their corresponding video content as self-supervision signals for video representation learning.
Video self-supervised learning methods should explain Wang et al.'s approach addressing self-supervised video representation learning through video pace prediction.

Video self-supervised learning methods should describe Diba et al.'s DynaMoNet approach focusing on SSL of motions in videos by employing dynamic motion filters to enhance motion representations for improving human action recognition.
Video self-supervised learning methods should explain that CoCLR bears similarities to SimCLR in the context of SSL with videos.

Video self-supervised learning methods should explain that auditory and visual components in videos are intrinsically interconnected and describe Korbar et al.'s self-supervised temporal synchronization approach to learn comprehensive models for both video and audio analysis.
Video self-supervised learning methods should mention that methodologies by Arandjelovic et al. are founded on joint video and audio modalities.
Video self-supervised learning methods should describe that certain studies incorporated both video and text modalities including Videobert, speech2action, and learning methods by Stroud et al.
Video self-supervised learning methods should explain Alayrac et al.'s tri-modal approach involving vision, audio, and language in videos.

Video self-supervised learning methods should describe Sermanet et al.'s self-supervised technique for learning representations and robotic behaviors from unlabeled videos captured from various viewpoints.

requirements.content: Video self-supervised learning methods should describe Wang et al.'s self-supervised algorithm for learning visual correspondence in unlabeled videos by utilizing cycle consistency in time as a self-supervised signal.
Video self-supervised learning methods should mention that extensions of Wang et al.'s work have been explored by Li et al. and Jabri et al.
Video self-supervised learning methods should explain Lai et al.'s MAST memory-augmented self-supervised method that enables generalizable and accurate pixel-level tracking.
Video self-supervised learning methods should describe Zhang et al.'s approach of employing spatial-temporal consistency of depth maps to mitigate forgetting during the learning process.
Video self-supervised learning methods should explain Zhao et al.'s video cloze procedure (VCP) algorithm that facilitates learning rich spatial-temporal representations for videos.

Video self-supervised learning methods should describe Feichtenhofer et al.'s extension of MAE to video representation learning demonstrating that leveraging naive ViT along with spatiotemporal co-occurrence of videos can outperform vanilla supervised training.
Video self-supervised learning methods should explain Gupta et al.'s Siamese approach demonstrating the importance of asymmetrically modeling the spatiotemporal information of videos.
